title: Login redirect loop for users with '+' in email or aliased emails after Okta-to-Entra-ID migration
problem: After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users experience an infinite redirect loop during login. Authentication at Entra ID succeeds, but upon redirect back to TaskFlow, the user is immediately sent back to Entra ID. The affected users correlate strongly with having plus signs in their email addresses (e.g., jane+taskflow@company.com) or having had their email changed/aliased during the migration.
root_cause_hypothesis: TaskFlow's authentication callback matches the email claim from the incoming OIDC/SAML token against stored user records, and this matching logic fails for certain email formats. Most likely: (1) Entra ID returns a differently-normalized form of the email than Okta did (e.g., Okta may have returned the alias/plus-tagged form while Entra returns the canonical address, or vice versa), or (2) TaskFlow's user lookup does not account for plus-addressing (the local-part after '+' is stripped or handled differently between providers), or (3) users whose emails were aliased during migration have a stored email in TaskFlow that no longer matches what Entra ID sends in the token. When the match fails, TaskFlow cannot establish a session and redirects back to the IdP. A stale half-created session cookie from the failed attempt exacerbates the loop, which is why incognito mode sometimes provides temporary relief.
reproduction_steps: Identify a user with a plus sign in their email address (e.g., user+tag@company.com) or one whose email was aliased during the Okta-to-Entra migration
Have that user attempt to log into TaskFlow via SSO
Observe successful authentication at Microsoft Entra ID
Observe redirect back to TaskFlow followed by immediate re-redirect to Entra ID, creating an infinite loop
Compare the email claim in the Entra ID token (decode the id_token JWT or check SAML assertion) against the email stored in TaskFlow's user database for that user
environment: TaskFlow with Microsoft Entra ID SSO (recently migrated from Okta). Affects all browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox). Not OS-specific. Approximately 30% of users affected, correlated with email format, not role or department.
impact: 30% of the user base cannot log into TaskFlow at all in their normal browser. The workaround (incognito mode) is fragile and temporary. This is a complete authentication failure for a significant portion of users following a production SSO migration.
recommended_fix: 1. **Immediate investigation:** Decode the id_token or SAML assertion from Entra ID for an affected user and compare the email/preferred_username/UPN claim against the email stored in TaskFlow's user table. Identify the exact mismatch. 2. **Fix the matching logic:** Normalize emails during the token-to-user matching step — lowercase comparison, handle plus-addressing consistently, and consider matching on a stable identifier (like OIDC `sub` claim or an immutable user ID) rather than email alone. 3. **Data migration:** For users whose stored email diverged during the Okta-to-Entra migration, update TaskFlow's user records to match what Entra ID now returns, or add support for multiple email aliases per user. 4. **Session cleanup:** Clear or invalidate any stale session cookies created by failed authentication attempts to stop the loop from self-reinforcing. 5. **Add logging:** Log the incoming token claims and the user lookup result in the auth callback so future mismatches are immediately visible.
proposed_test_case: Create test users with emails containing plus signs (user+tag@domain.com), mixed-case emails (User@Domain.com), and emails that were changed/aliased (old@domain.com → new@domain.com with old stored in DB). Run each through the SSO authentication flow and verify that (a) the token email claim is correctly matched to the stored user record, (b) a valid session is created, and (c) no redirect loop occurs.
information_gaps: The exact claim Entra ID is sending (email vs. preferred_username vs. UPN) — this needs to be confirmed by decoding an actual token
Whether TaskFlow matches on email alone or also uses a subject/user ID claim
The exact email values stored in TaskFlow's database for affected users vs. what Entra ID returns
Whether the stale cookie issue is a separate bug or just a symptom of the matching failure
